author: Dr. Rebecca Chen, MD (Pulmonary fellow) / Dr. Anil Vachani, MD (Pulmonary attending)
content:
PULMONARY CONSULTATION — MICU
Date/Time: 2/18/2027, 3:30 PM (Hospital Day 4)
Patient: Nguyen, Lan T (MRN-90215888)
Consulting team: MICU (Dr. Patel resident, Dr. Foster attending)
Interpreter: Vietnamese language line for any patient communication; patient currently intubated and sedated.

REASON FOR CONSULT:
(1) Management of moderate ARDS in setting of severe biliary pancreatitis / septic shock; (2) interpretation of an incidental right-upper-lobe pulmonary nodule seen on 2/16 admission CXR and reconfirmed on today's review.

CURRENT STATUS:
Ms. Nguyen was electively intubated last night (2/17 around 11 PM) for worsening respiratory failure on HFNC — RR 32, SpO2 88% on FiO2 0.7, increasing work of breathing. Patient and family had been pre-counseled re: anticipated intubation course with Vietnamese interpreter. Currently on volume-controlled ventilation, lung-protective settings: Vt 320 mL (6 mL/kg PBW), PEEP 12, FiO2 0.55, RR 22. P/F ratio on most recent ABG ~140 — moderate ARDS by Berlin criteria. Plateau pressure 24, driving pressure 12.

Sepsis: weaning off norepinephrine; currently 0.01 mcg/kg/min. Lactate 1.4 (cleared). WBC trending down 14.2 (from peak 19.8). On pip/tazo + vancomycin pending biliary culture sensitivities.

Pancreatitis: Lipase trending down 612 (from peak 1124). General surgery rounding daily; cholecystectomy deferred until clinically stable.

AKI: Cr 2.4 today, peaked at 2.6 yesterday. UOP adequate at 0.6-0.8 mL/kg/h.

IMAGING REVIEW (with attending dictation):
- Admission CXR (2/15 PM, ED): No focal infiltrates. Mild bibasilar atelectasis. No nodule clearly visible on PA view (somewhat under-penetrated).
- AM CXR 2/16 (~ 9 AM): Bilateral interstitial markings developing, R > L. Small bilateral pleural effusions. **RUL nodule, 2.5 cm, well-circumscribed, somewhat eccentric in shape; not seen on the prior 2/15 ED CXR but the ED film was under-penetrated.** No mediastinal widening. No cavitation. No air bronchograms within or around the nodule.
- AM CXR 2/17 (~ 6 AM): Worsening bilateral interstitial markings consistent with ARDS. Same RUL nodule unchanged. Effusions slightly increased.
- AM CXR 2/18 (today, ~ 8 AM, post-intubation): Endotracheal tube tip 4 cm above carina, NG tube in good position. Bilateral interstitial markings stable. Right pleural effusion slightly increased. Bilateral basilar atelectasis from ventilation. **RUL nodule again visualized — borders well-defined, 2.5 cm.**

FINDINGS ON CHEST PHYSICAL EXAM:
Intubated, sedated. Lung auscultation: scattered crackles diffusely bilateral, more pronounced at bases and right anterior chest. Endotracheal tube secured at 22 cm at the teeth. Right chest with mild dullness to percussion (effusion). Chest expansion symmetric. No subcutaneous emphysema.

IMPRESSION:

#1 MODERATE ARDS (Berlin criteria, P/F 140) — etiology presumed pancreatitis-associated and/or sepsis-associated. Lung-protective ventilation in place. Consider prone positioning if P/F < 150 sustained on FiO2 ≥0.6.

#2 INCIDENTAL 2.5 cm RIGHT UPPER LOBE PULMONARY NODULE — newly identified on admission imaging during sepsis workup. Has remained unchanged in size and shape across three serial CXRs over 48 hours. Important features:
- Size: 2.5 cm — falls into the intermediate-to-large category by Fleischner-society standards; pulmonary nodules >8 mm warrant further evaluation.
- Margins: well-circumscribed on CXR; finer margin characterization (spiculation, lobulation) requires dedicated CT.
- Location: right upper lobe — concerning given the higher prevalence of malignancy in upper lobes.
- Density: cannot characterize as solid vs subsolid vs ground-glass from CXR alone.
- Stability: no prior chest imaging is available at this institution for direct comparison. Prior CXR from her 2010 LTBI workup at Hopkins Bayview was reviewed — no nodule visible at that time, but image quality and 17-year gap limit reassurance.

DIFFERENTIAL FOR THIS NODULE (pulmonary perspective):
- Primary lung malignancy — must be considered given size, upper-lobe location, age, ex-smoker status (14 pack-years; quit 2004), strong family history of lung cancer (brother d. 3/2026 of squamous cell).
- Granuloma — possible given documented latent TB infection (positive PPD 2010, never treated) and Vietnamese origin (TB-endemic region of birth). Could represent old, calcified granuloma or active disease.
- Active tuberculosis or other mycobacterial infection — must rule out given LTBI history.
- Endemic fungal infection (histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis, blastomycosis) — less likely epidemiologically but consider in Vietnamese-immigrant population. Cryptococcal pulmonary infection a consideration in immunocompromised hosts (she is not significantly immunocompromised at baseline, though acutely septic).
- Metastasis — possible but less likely without known primary; will defer to oncologic workup if biopsy malignant.
- Hamartoma or other benign lesion — possible but size argues against pure benign incidentaloma.
- Rheumatologic / autoimmune nodule (RA, GPA, etc.) — no known autoimmune disease; clinical context does not support; consider but lower on differential.

RECOMMENDATIONS:

A) IMMEDIATE (this admission):
1. Rule out active tuberculosis. ID consult — recommend 3 sputum AFB smears and 1 sputum NAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF) given documented LTBI history and Vietnamese origin. We do not want to delay other care including ICU procedures.
2. Once ARDS picture has improved enough to permit transport, obtain a dedicated chest CT with thin sections (1 mm slices through the nodule) to better characterize. Given iodinated-contrast allergy, can perform without contrast for nodule characterization purposes. Alternatively, contrast can be given with the standard 13-7-1 hour pre-medication regimen if also assessing mediastinal nodes.
3. Consider PET-CT for FDG avidity — schedule outpatient if biopsy not pursued inpatient.

B) MEDIUM-TERM:
1. Pulmonary clinic follow-up post-discharge in 4 weeks if not biopsied inpatient.
2. If non-invasive workup remains indeterminate, transthoracic CT-guided needle biopsy is the most likely next step (pulmonary nodule clinic referral). Given the patient's prolonged ICU course and likely deconditioning, this can be planned for outpatient pending clinical recovery.
3. Anticoagulation considerations: she is at risk for procedure-related bleeding and is on heparin VTE prophylaxis (per ICU; pharmacologic now restarted with stable platelets). If she develops VTE during this admission she will need therapeutic anticoagulation, which would impact biopsy timing.

C) ARDS / VENTILATOR MANAGEMENT:
1. Continue lung-protective settings: Vt 6 mL/kg PBW, PEEP titrated for oxygenation and compliance, plateau pressure goal <30, driving pressure goal <14.
2. If P/F < 150 sustained, initiate prone positioning protocol (16 hours daily).
3. Conservative fluid management — no further bolus unless hypotensive.
4. Daily SBT once sedation lightened.
5. Anticipate possible prolonged ventilation given severity.

Thank you for this consultation. Will see in follow-up tomorrow with the ID team to coordinate TB workup.
attestation:
Electronically signed by:
Dr. Rebecca Chen, MD (Pulmonary fellow) — primary author
Dr. Anil Vachani, MD (Attending Pulmonologist) — reviewing attending
Date: 2/18/2027
